channel.title: Stories by Teri Radichel on Medium
channel.description: Stories by Teri Radichel on Medium

image.title: Stories by Teri Radichel on Medium

item.title: Reducing token burn, Anthropic Mythos, How I use AI for pentesting
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
Latest stories on AI, vibe coding, AI security, and AI for security research

I recently spoke at the Computer History Museum in Mountain View at AWS [Security] Community Day. That conference has expanded beyond security but it was still heavily security-focused which I love. Thanks so much to @john.varghese and AWS for helping me get back there again. I’ve added a link to my slides on How I Use AI For Penetration Testing here:

How I Use AI for Penetration Testing

You probably heard all about Mythos from Anthropic if you are even remotely following AI or security topics. I wrote a bit about that here which is that we cannot really know anything about it until we can actually try it. But the larger question I have has to do with the viability of relying on AI models for business given their transient and non-transparent nature. The inability to rely on value per token is a problem.

Anthropic Mythos

One of the issues I was having related to the whole Anthropic Opus 4.6 meltdown that affected an inconsistent number of people recently — and seems to be heavily skewed to security research users — was implementation of the Yubikey authentication architecture I’ve been working on and wrote about here:

Yubikey Push To Run A Lambda Function

I dove into some of the details of a Yubikey authentication system in this post:

Where are the Private Bits of FIDO2 Compliant Passkey Xtored?

I took some time off from working on that hoping Anthropic would figure out whatever nerfed Opus 4.6. Now I’m back at it and have extended my Lambda Troubleshooter in an attempt to quickly resolve problems with this and all future Lambda architectures. The larger point here is how effective use of AI non-deterministic solutions in combination with deterministic solutions can slow your token burn rate and help you achieve ROI with AI solutions.

Reducing Token Burn Rate With A Well-Designed Architecture

Subscribe on Substack for more posts and more timely updates.

Teri Radichel :: Security and Technology Research | Substack

— Teri Radichel

Reducing token burn, Anthropic Mythos, How I use AI for pentesting was originally published in Cloud Security on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: How I Use AI for Penetration Testing
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
Speaking at the Computer History Museum in Mountain View, CA April 10, 2026

I’m honored to be speaking at the AWS Security Community Day again this year. Come have a chat about the impact of AI on penetration testing, bug bounties, and learn about how I use AI for pentesting.

Here’s a teaser…

Register here:

AWS Community Day, 2026

See you!

— Teri Radichel

New Blog:

Teri Radichel :: Security and Technology Research | Substack

How I Use AI for Penetration Testing was originally published in Cloud Security on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: AI and Security Research
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
How secure are your agents? Does vibe coding really work? How can I get started? What’s an agent and how do I create one? What you still need to know if you’re vibe coding.

🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/

I’ve published this before but I’m now blogging over at Substack. Here are some of my recent stories. Stories are free for first 30 days. Subscribe and follow the sections that interest you.

  • What I’ve vibe coded in the past 2.5 weeks that I couldn’t get done previously in like 4 years.
  • A multi-agent work flow including security considerations, running things sequentially or in parallel
  • KISS — Keep It Simple Silly — to improve AI coding results
  • Securing custom agents — lots of considerations
  • I’m currently working on an updated version of kicking off batch jobs with MFA — or more specifically, a Yubikey. Currently my two factors are IP address and a Yubikey but I’ll probably add a password into the mix later.
  • I showed how to set up a secure pentest environment for Burp Collaborator
  • I create a script for finding rogue TLS traffic based on Certificate Fingerprints.
  • There’s a post on spotting a Man In The Middle Attack on AWS which is not always going to show up in VPC flow logs…

I have a section for those who are less technical now called Cybersecurity Me, or anyone trying ot improve personal cybersecurity.

  • One day I logged into my Fidelity account and CoPilot was connected — what? I have never used CoPilot.
  • Beware of what OpenClaw can do if you use it …
  • Got tired of phone scammers on my phone — here’s how you can report them
  • Does your bank support Yubikeys? They should. Not Passkeys. Yubikeys.

Wrote some posts on security bugs. Expect more in this category soon.

  • Took a look at a “vulnerability” in Amazon Bedrock when creating agents in a sandbox
  • I showed how to find security bugs with AI while coding
  • Took a look at which programming languages are best at preventing serialization bugs that lead to vulnerabilities
  • Where are the crytographic bits stored when you register a FIDO compliant passkey — it depends
  • I create a script that monitors network traffic including showing you the executables that are generating that traffic
  • Took a look at what caused the Lexus Nexus Breach
  • Dove into an authentication architecture and things you should be asking your vendors about that

I have links to all my class materials over there. May migrate everything to that platform in the future.

Then there’s my link to get the code on GitHub currently…which may move in the future.

If you are just getting started with AI coding here’s how:

Implement A Simple Project With Kiro CLI and a README.md

Subscribe at this link and follow the sections that interest you most.

teriradichel.substack.com

— Teri Radichel

item.title: 2.5 Weeks of Vibe Coding
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
Compared to similar code I tried to implement for years to deploy an AWS environment for running batch jobs (as AI agents or not). How I did it.

On March 7th, 2026 I published a post about creating a way to push a Yubikey to run a job. As always one thing led to another and here I am again, essentially trying to set up a common infrastructure to execute batch jobs (or if you must, AI agents, though not all my jobs use AI).

In this post I’ll tell you what I have accomplished since that date as of today, March 24th in the wee hours of the morning. That’s 17 days later. About 2 and a half weeks of vibe coding. I have a reusable bootstrap script that deploys an AWS environment in an OU with multiple accounts and resources. Skip to the bottom of the post for the full list of resources.

What I’ve Vibe Coded In 2.5 Weeks

On March 7th, 2026 I published a post about creating a way to push a Yubikey to run a job. As always one thing led to another and here I am again, essentially trying to set up a common infrastructure to execute batch jobs (or if you must, AI agents) …

This is essentially an implementation of the security automation framework I have been trying to complete for like four years here

Read full story

Subscribe to my Blog and follow Good Vibes for more stories like this.

— Teri Radichel

item.title: AI in a Lambda Function, Amazon Bedrock Agents, Cookie Cutter Vibe Coding, Finding Security Bugs…
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
AI in a Lambda Function, Amazon Bedrock Agents, Cookie Cutter Vibe Coding, Finding Security Bugs with AI

Stories this week published to the Substack blog — teriradichel.substack.com

🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/

I’m finding that Substack is not as good at SEO as Medium. If you don’t already have a following it may be easier for you to get started on Medium. But overall I’m liking the functionality that Substack has to offer. For those not following me over there yet here are some of the latest stories.

I tried out AI in a Lambda function (Querying CloudTrail Logs and getting Recommendations to Fix Them), played around with an Amazon Bedrock agent, and showed how to create a simple AWS Kiro CLI project with a README.md file. I also explain how to create cookie cutter projects to save on tokens.

AWS pushes steering docs for Kiro but I’m having decent success with a simple README.md file for my purposes. I might not be doing what everyone else is doing but for me this simple approach is working well so far.

I also wrote about a number of strategies for using AI to find security bugs for those who are interested in such things.

My lab code for classes is now on GitHub and I explain some of the things I attempted to do to minimize the risks associated with giving students access to cloud resources in classes.

I’ve also got a link to my GitHub posts in the old-school looking CODE category.

You can follow for free or subscribe and choose only the categories which interest you rather than everything if some of the topics do not pertain to you.

Enjoy!

— Teri Radichel

teriradichel.substack.com

Teri Radichel :: Security and Technology Research | Substack

AI in a Lambda Function, Amazon Bedrock Agents, Cookie Cutter Vibe Coding, Finding Security Bugs… was originally published in Cloud Security on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: The most important word you need to know about AI, traffic not visible in network logs, securing…
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
The most important word you need to know about AI, traffic not visible in network logs, securing your pentest network, and phone scams

Stories published this week ending February 13, 2026

🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/

As most of you know I’m primarily publishing on substack now. You can follow me over there for the most recent stores. I’ll still publish a few here and there on Medium. The cool thing about Substack is you can subscribe to the sections I write about that interest you most instead of getting everything I write. I realize some people are more interested in AI than security and vice versa so subscribe to whatever applies to you in particular over there.

Here are the stories I published this week:

Non-Deterministic

The most important word you need to understand about AI

https://teriradichel.substack.com/p/non-deterministic

Non-Deterministic

Report Phone Scams to the FTC

Fake calls about loans in progress exist all over the BBB scam website but that is not where to report them to get them to stop

https://teriradichel.substack.com/p/report-phone-scams-to-the-ftc

Report Phone Scams to the FTC

How To Spot Impostor Traffic When The Network Logs Look Correct

How can you tell if someone spoofed an IP address and has compromised encryption in transit?

https://teriradichel.substack.com/p/how-spot-impostor-traffic-when-the

How To Spot Impostor Traffic When The Network Logs Look Correct

Spotting a Man In the Middle Attack on AWS in Network Logs

When your IP logs will tell you that your traffic is being intercepted and when they won’t

https://teriradichel.substack.com/p/spotting-a-man-in-the-middle-attack

Spotting a Man In the Middle Attack on AWS in Network Logs

Securing Pentest and Bug Bounty Research Networks on AWS

Make it harder for an attacker to compromise your pentest infrastructure and snipe your bounties

https://teriradichel.substack.com/p/securing-pentest-and-bug-bounty-research

Securing Pentest and Bug Bounty Research Networks on AWS

https://teriradichel.substack.com/

The most important word you need to know about AI, traffic not visible in network logs, securing… was originally published in Cloud Security on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

item.title: Mission Accomplished — A Post in Every Security Section
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
Mission Accomplished — A Post in Every Security Section

(Except the get the code section coming later)

Mission accomplished. I have a post in every section on the new blog. Except the last one where I’m going to hopefully publish actual code — that’s coming later for various reasons. One of the cool things on substack is that you can follow only the sections that interest you most.

One of the sections is for less technical users and people who want to secure their own assets. I created that section because invariably I’m giving a presentation on something unrelated and people just want to know how to “secure their own stuff.” I also get questions from my Mom who says she has no idea what I’m talking about. I’m going to try to keep it simpler in that section and explain technical things hopefully in terms anyone can understand, but maybe just on the edge of getting technical for those who need and want that information.

That section is called Cybersecure Me and I just wrote a post about Bank Accounts and Yubikeys which seems to be pretty popular.

The other section I hadn’t yet published in is called Security Insight. This is a catch all category for people who work for organizations trying to reduce cybersecurity risk. I might analyze breaches or provide insights that help companies lower their risk by taking various actions. This category might also be interesting for those who invest in cybersecurity products.

I just posted about Using AI to Analyze Network Packets which I think is going to be a game change for defenders working in SOCs. As a side note I gripe about products and companies spamming the Internet with packets in the name of security.

Overall I think this blog is more representative of me at the current moment than my existing Medium blog. It hits the categories I want to talk about more directly and it targets the different groups of people who have different interests a bit better. We’ll see how it works out.

Subscribe for free. Posts will end up behind a paywall after 30 days the way I currently have it configured.

https://teriradichel.substack.com

item.title: Good Vibes and Security Insight
item.description:
Remember to subscribe (for free) on substack for more content!

item.title: Writing Penetration Test Reports

A query from a client got me thinking about this today

Continue reading on Cloud Security »
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel

item.title: Teri Radichel on Substack
item.dc:creator: Teri Radichel
item.content:encoded:
Pondering publishing on Substack — I did it…

🦊 New Blog — Security and AI Research: https://teriradichel.substack.com/

I’m pondering publishing on Substack so if you are following here you might want to sign up on my Stubstack feed.

Teri Radichel :: Security and Technology Research | Substack

And now, I need your help. I would like to know if there is any interest in any of the following services.

One of the things I’m pondering doing on Substack is a Q & A service where people can submit written questions and I will answer them if I know the answer or possibly research the answers or get help from other experts if I don’t.

Is this something people would be willing to pay for? What are your questions? What if you could expense the subscription to your company?

The founding tier is more expensive because that is the tier at which you can ask and I will answer questions. Other tiers can read the Q & A.

I’m not sure I’m actually going to do this without some additional research. Let me know what information you need and are willing to pay for by signing up for my Substack feed and dropping me a line.

Note that I won’t blatantly promote products I don’t use or spend a lot of time for a small amount of money doing a lot of legwork to research and market a product. I’m not an influencer. But if there was a substantial subscription from a company and its employees I would use and write about a product — honestly. I could try it out and write about my experience using it as I have written about many other products in this blog.

I will not write exactly what a company tells me to write or deal with editors from the company other than basic spelling and grammar. I have not had good experiences with a lot of editors (except the editor for my book — who was amazing.) Not a fan of people rewriting my work to say something other than I intended to say.

What about materials for educators teaching classes? Do you need help?

Anyway, I’m not sure what I’m going to do just yet. I’ll keep you posted here. But sign up on Substack for both free and paid posts and let me know if you have any interest in any particular paid content.

I like Medium, but the problem is I make absolutely no money here so I cannot justify a ton of time. That’s why my posts have dropped off a bit. I am also hesitant to share proprietary research I’m working on through my business but with paid subscriptions — maybe? I wish there was a way to also allow subscriptions on GitHub and I could write and share code more easily. Pondering ideas.

Drop me a line on LinkedIn if you have any particular needs you’d be willing to pay for that would be worth my time to set up over there and I’ll do it. If not, I’ll just keep posting here or there infrequently. Or both.

Follow here — and on Substack — for more.

Teri Radichel :: Security and Technology Research | Substack

Teri Radichel | © 2nd Sight Lab 2025
